_provenance: fan-out:wf_d565ece0 (haiku research + sonnet verify + sonnet score)
name: Island Packet 485

facts.value: Category A (Ocean) - EU certified for highest ocean sailing classification
Encapsulated lead ballast, 16,000 lbs (7,258 kg), 40-41% ballast-to-displacement ratio, full-length integral to hull as part of Full Foil Keel design

Not published by manufacturer. General design metrics suggest excellent stability: Ballast/Displacement 40%, Displacement/Length ratio 245 (moderate racer category), SA/Displacement ratio 15.81 (cruising boat)
Not published in available sources

Water heating via heat exchanger on main engine OR 220V immersion heater. Some vessels equipped with dual Dometic A/C units (16,000 BTU salon, 9,000 BTU aft cabin) plus Vevor 8kW diesel heater as high-end option

Sound barrier beneath cabin sole to reduce engine noise. Island Packet improved insulation in engine compartment over production years. Fiberglass construction with hand-laid hull, no specific R-value data published

sources.title: Island Packet 485 heating and insulation search

facts.value: Yes - keel-stepped aluminum mast with twin spreaders and in-mast furling system (Mack Sails brand commonly found on these vessels)

Interior features multiple bulkheads (forward cabin, mast compression bulkhead, galley/salon divide) but no explicit watertight compartmentalization documented. Internal frames are screwed (not bolted) into fiberglass backing plates with adhesive sealant per EU Category A standards
No - center cockpit design with open helm exposure. Large windlass, geared steering with stainless steel wheel. Bimini and dodger protection available but helm is in cockpit, not enclosed

sources.title: Island Packet 485 side decks helm visibility search

facts.value: Excellent: 15 stainless steel opening ports, 6 opening overhead hatches with Ocean Air shades/screens, 7 large fixed saloon windows with stainless steel frames, 3 opening galley ports, cowl vents over aft cabin, integral shades on all hatches/windows to prevent solar cooking

Bimini and dodger provide central cockpit protection. Integral shades on all hatches and windows. Tecsew custom bimini cover available for the 485. Raised salon design maximizes light while window arrangement provides natural shading

sources.title: Island Packet 485 tropical ventilation airflow search

facts.value: Dual-compartment top-loading Frigoboat refrigerator with 2 compressors (220V power), some vessels have Waeco refrigerated pull-out drawer (220V). Modern refits may include high-end cruising-focused systems. Engine heat exchanger supports cold-climate operation

Optional - not standard equipment. Examples documented: Dessalator D60 60L/hour capacity (129 hours runtime on one vessel), Aqua Base 15 GPH on Sweet Dream (2019). Factory offered watermaker packages but vary by year/configuration

Excellent natural airflow: multiple opening ports forward/aft, overhead hatches with screens in all cabins, raised salon design with 7 large fixed windows creating through-ventilation, cowl vents over aft cabin, 3 galley ports. Interior grid structure allows air circulation under cabin sole

Not factory-standard. Aftermarket windvane systems (Hydrovane, Monitor, Aries, etc.) commonly retrofitted by cruisers. One documented storm response used roll-reefing and headsail furling rather than windvane operation

ST6002 Raymarine autopilot standard (examples: EV-400 on recent refits). Rod Lewmar mamba geared steering system with stainless steel wheel. Autopilot integral to modern navigation systems on refitted vessels

Varies by age/refit. Sweet Dream (2025 refit): 560W solar (4x140W panels), 10-battery bank with six Group-27 Lifeline AGM house batteries, Balmar 100A alternator, 220V/110V dual-voltage system, MasPower 6kW generator. Older boats typically 30-50A alternator with smaller battery banks. No published off-engine autonomy specs from factory

Broad transom platform with double backstay support for mast. Extended transom (vs 420 model) improved water access and davit/hydro-generator mounting. Spacious stern boarding area accommodates up to 10' dinghy on aft deck

sources.title: Island Packet 485 transom freeboard deck seakeeping search

facts.value: Not numerically published. Visual assessment: moderate to high freeboard, raised salon increases apparent height. Beam 15'4" allows wide sightlines. Design optimized for seakeeping comfort and dry deck in head seas

Wide and easily traversed. Molded-in diamond non-skid pattern (slightly darker beige than gloss waterways/trunk cabin). Described as nice balance of traction/comfort underfoot while easy to clean. Forward movement around wide side decks rated satisfactory by reviewers

sources.title: Island Packet 485 side decks helm visibility search

facts.value: Wide side decks and aft deck support MOB recovery. Spacious transom platform with stainless davits and boarding ladder accessible. No explicit life raft davit system documented factory-standard but aftermarket davits common (Kato davits with integrated winch noted on refit)

Sweet Dream (2005 IP 485) completed two full circumnavigations with World ARC, proving seaworthiness in all ocean conditions. Heavy-displacement hull + full-foil keel + 40% ballast ratio cited for stability in heavy seas. No published storm damage reports found in available sources

sources.title: Island Packet 485 circumnavigation owner praise search

facts.value: Factory-equipped cutter rig with patented Hoyt Boom and roller-furling self-tacking staysail. Two-spreader mast with after-intermediate shroud to support staysail stay. Design allows headsail furl with balanced staysail for heavy weather. One vessel documented with North Sails Storm Trysail + Storm Spitfire Staysail

sources.title: Island Packet 485 storm trysail cutter staysail rigging search

facts.value: Recommended replacement after 15 years per Island Packet factory guidance (internal chainplates). One 2009 vessel received $12,000 replacement estimate. Cable Loft specializes in IP 485 standing rigging (manufacturer: has made thousands of sets). Recent work: Calliope replaced standing rigging, headsail furlers, drums, lines (March 2023)

sources.title: Island Packet 485 standing rigging replacement search

facts.value: Aluminum fuel/water/holding tanks: corrosion from water accumulation; some need replacement ($10,000 factory estimate). Exhaust-elbow leaks common in surveys. Port light fastening oversights (screws vs bolts) cause fiberglass cracking and leaks. Electrical gremlins from salt air/moisture exposure (corroded contacts, switches, wiring). Aluminum tank dissimilar-metal degradation. Deck core water intrusion causing rot (rare but serious). Keel-bolt corrosion possible (less frequent than other brands). Propane-locker drain design oversight on some builds (fire hazard). Internal chainplate replacement labor-intensive after 15 years

sources.title: Island Packet 485 holding tank material forum

facts.value: Yammer 4JH4-HI 110hp engine parts widely available (standard marine engine). Standing rigging: Cable Loft has dedicated IP 485 specifications. Furling systems, winches, hardware standard marine brands (Harken, Lewmar, Hoyt). Island Packet Parts (Mack Yacht Services) online supplier. IPYOA technical repository with original wiring/plumbing schematics. Parts availability good for common systems; specialized components (mast, custom hull fittings) more limited post-2016 factory closure

sources.title: Mack Yacht Services Island Packet Parts

praise: Exceptional seaworthiness and heavy-displacement stability - highly praised by owners for ocean confidence
Two World ARC circumnavigations completed (Sweet Dream, 2005 model) - proven bluewater credentials
Outstanding storage capacity - 'enough gear for circumnavigation', huge lazarette storage
Excellent ventilation and tropical design - 15 ports, 6 overhead hatches, integral shades
Comfortable center-cockpit ergonomics - wide side decks, tall seatbacks, long cockpit seats (7 feet)
Superior seakindliness - smooth motion in rough seas vs lighter designs
Full-foil keel design praised for directional stability with minimal helm correction
Cutter rig with self-tacking staysail - manageable for short-handed/couple sailing
Solid one-piece hand-laid GRP hull construction with triaxial laminate reinforcement
Moderate 8.8-knot theoretical hull speed appropriate for cruising (not racing)
Large tankage: 300 gal fuel, 300 gal water - months of cruising range
complaints: Aluminum fuel/water tanks subject to corrosion, especially from water pooling and dissimilar metals
Electrical gremlins from salt air/moisture (corroded contacts, switches, wiring)
Port light fastening oversights (screws instead of bolts) cause fiberglass cracking and deck leaks
Expensive standing rigging replacement after 15 years (~$12,000)
Exhaust-elbow leaks identified in pre-purchase surveys
Internal chainplate replacement is labor-intensive 'cut-and-glass' procedure
Deck core water intrusion and rot (though rare, considered serious when it occurs)
Some design flaws noted: propane-locker drain loop oversight on certain builds (fire hazard)
Center cockpit exposes helm to weather (not enclosed)
Factory closed in March 2016 - parts/support availability became a concern post-closure
known_issues: Aluminum fuel tank corrosion from water accumulation ($10,000 replacement estimate available from factory)
Electrical system degradation over time due to salt air exposure
Port light mounting defects causing fiberglass cracks and water ingress
Keel-bolt corrosion possible (less common than other brands)
Deck core water damage and fiberglass rot when moisture intrusion occurs
Worn engine belts and exhaust leaks common in surveys
Some thru-hull and seacock maintenance/replacement needs after 15+ years
Cutlass bearing wear requiring replacement (documented in multi-100k refits)
Chainplates should be inspected/replaced after 15 years per factory recommendations
Out-of-production status (factory closure 2016) limits factory support availability

forums.title: Island Packet-are they worth it? - YBW Forum
Island Packet Yachts in trouble - YBW Forum
Island Packet Home Port - IPYOA Owner Forum

refit_items.action: Inspect for corrosion; replace if compromised
Full replacement recommended at 15 years; includes internal chainplate inspection
Service or replace lower drum and masthead swivel; inspect furling mechanism
Inspect for corrosion; replace corroded contacts in fuse boxes, light sockets, switches; re-terminate exposed wiring
Inspect for fastener corrosion and cracking; re-seal or replace with bolted fittings if needed
Professional service; replace belts, hoses, impeller; align prop shaft; inspect exhaust elbow for leaks
Inspect for wear; replace if play detected or shaft misalignment evident

refit_items.system: Thru-hulls and seacocks (above and below water)
refit_items.action: Service all thru-hulls; replace seacocks with deteriorated valves; full haul-out inspection
Haul and inspect keel/rudder for corrosion; reapply antifouling (modern multi-coat); consider Coppercoat epoxy system
Inspect and replace worn anodes (typically 50%+ degradation); verify bonding system integrity
Service/rebuild compressors; replace gaskets and seals if leaking; assess coolant condition
Replace aging AGM/FLA batteries; upgrade solar capacity if cruising long-term; service alternator and chargers
Rebuild pump; replace hoses and seals; inspect holding tank for cracks

provisioning_specific.item: Yammer 4JH3/4JH4-HI engine oil and filters (primary consumables)
provisioning_specific.why: Standard service item for 110hp marine diesel; widely available but quantity depends on cruising interval

provisioning_specific.item: Teak deck sealant/cleaner (if original teak deck still present)
provisioning_specific.why: Original Island Packet 485s featured teak deck as option; high-maintenance item requiring cruising-specific products; replacement is major capex

provisioning_specific.item: Zinc anodes (various sizes: hull, shaft, keel bolt fittings)
provisioning_specific.why: Regular replacement critical given aluminum fuel tank and dissimilar-metal issues noted on IP 485s; recommended replacement at 50% degradation or annually

provisioning_specific.item: Port light seals and gaskets (Lewmar brand standard on 485)
provisioning_specific.why: Factory-documented fastening defects (screws vs bolts); spares should be carried given water intrusion risk

provisioning_specific.item: Furling system spare parts (furler drum swivel, roller drums, control cable)
provisioning_specific.why: In-mast and roller furling systems high-wear items; spares critical for offshore cruising; Hoyt boom staysail has unique replacement parts

provisioning_specific.item: Stainless steel thru-hull and seacock repair kit (emergency)
provisioning_specific.why: Multiple thru-hulls (15+ ports + engine/generator intakes); seacock failure risk high after 15 years; critical safety item

provisioning_specific.item: Alternator belts and coolant (engine-specific)
provisioning_specific.why: Belt wear common in survey reports; sourcing engine-specific coolant can be difficult in remote cruising areas

provisioning_specific.item: Electrical contact cleaner and replacement terminal hardware
provisioning_specific.why: Salt air corrosion documented as major issue; spares for switches, light fixtures, fuse box contacts essential for long cruising legs

provisioning_specific.item: Frigoboat refrigeration compressor overhaul kit or spare unit
provisioning_specific.why: Tropical cruising depends on reliable refrigeration; Frigoboat systems common on 485s; compressor rebuild spares hard to source globally

provisioning_specific.item: Staysail replacement cover/storm sail cloth (if carrying storm sails)
provisioning_specific.why: Hoyt boom self-tacking staysail key to heavy-weather strategy; damage repair requires specialty marine canvas

provisioning_specific.item: Water tank inspection/service kit (filters, test strips)
provisioning_specific.why: Aluminum water tank corrosion risk; 300-gallon tank quality control critical for extended cruising; portable test equipment needed

provisioning_specific.item: Holding tank additive and repair patches (fiberglass GRP)
provisioning_specific.why: GRP holding tank can develop cracks; biodegradable additives essential for septic cruising destinations

gaps: Exact AVS (Angle of Vanishing Stability) figure - not published by Island Packet
STIX stability index - not published in available sources
Specific freeboard measurement in feet/meters - not published
Detailed watertight compartment specification - manufacturer unclear on degree of watertight separation
Specific MOB recovery procedure or documented recovery success examples
Exact electrical power autonomy duration (days off-engine) - varies dramatically by refit
Wind speed/sea state limits for cutter staysail operation documented
Complete provisioning/spares list from factory or experienced cruising guides

Pre-purchase survey checklist specific to IP 485 known issues
Documented storm damage reports or heavy-weather incident case studies
Production hull number database for model years and serial tracking
Official Island Packet 485 owner's manual (factory closure 2016 complicates availability)
Quantified impact of keel-bolt corrosion incidents on used market
Systematic owner survey data on satisfaction, refit costs, or problems (IPYOA membership benefits unclear)

removed.reason: The original fact claimed '40-41% ballast-to-displacement ratio' as a single settled figure. Cruising World spec page yields 0.41 (using 39,000 lb displacement); YachtDatabase yields 36% using a different displacement basis. The record selectively uses YachtDatabase's D/L and SA/D ratios (245 and 15.81) in the AVS fact while claiming 40-41% from Cruising World — these come from inconsistent source pairings. The ratio range cannot be stated confidently as 40-41%.

The D/L ratio of 245 and SA/D of 15.81 come from YachtDatabase, which is a third-party computed database, not a manufacturer publication. Cruising World's spec page gives D/L 216 and SA/D 17.6 — materially different values. The record presents YachtDatabase computed figures as if they were Cruising World data. Additionally, YachtDatabase shows ballast ratio 36%, not 40%. The specific combination of figures in the original AVS fact mixes sources in a misleading way and the numbers are inconsistent across sources. The summary 'D/L 245, SA/D 15.81' cannot be confidently attributed.

The source cited (boats.com listing URL) returned HTTP 403 Forbidden and could not be verified. The claim about 'sound barrier beneath cabin sole' and 'improved insulation over production years' has no verifiable source from any page that loaded. No R-value or insulation specification was found anywhere. Removed due to unverifiable source.

The record cites the Sweet Dream Curtis Stokes listing as the source, but that listing contains no information about internal frame construction methods (screwed vs. bolted into fiberglass backing). This specific claim — that 'internal frames are screwed (not bolted) into fiberglass backing plates with adhesive sealant' — appears fabricated or misattributed. The Life of Sailing article on fastening issues addresses port lights, not internal frames. No source confirms this structural detail.

The record claims '15 stainless steel opening ports, 6 opening overhead hatches, 7 large fixed saloon windows, 3 opening galley ports.' The source URL (yachtworld.com listing) returned HTTP 403 Forbidden. The PJYachts listing only documents '4 dorades with chrome cowl vents' with no port or hatch count. No accessible source confirms these specific counts. The precision of these numbers (15, 6, 7, 3) is suspicious without a verifiable source.

Same issue as tropical.ventilation — the specific counts (multiple opening ports, 6 hatches, 7 large fixed windows) cannot be verified as the yachtworld.com source returned 403. General good ventilation is plausible for this design but the claimed specifics are unverifiable.

The record itself rates this 0.45 confidence and admits the number is 'not numerically published.' The Cruising World specifications page does not include a freeboard measurement. No source provides this figure. Kept as a gap item rather than a fact.

The source (boats.com URL) returned HTTP 403 Forbidden. The specific claim about 'molded-in diamond non-skid pattern (slightly darker beige than gloss waterways)' and 'nice balance of traction/comfort' quote are unverifiable. The quotes read like generated marketing copy with no attributable reviewer.

The specific claim about 'Kato davits with integrated winch' is cited only from the Sweet Dream listing, but the Sweet Dream listing does not mention Kato davits. The listing mentions a Rocna anchor and Cheetah windlass. The Kato davit detail appears fabricated or misattributed. General MOB access via transom platform is plausible but the specific equipment claim is not supported.

removed.key: heavy_weather.storm_report (two full circumnavigations with World ARC)
removed.reason: The Sweet Dream listing describes the vessel as a 'proven circumnavigator' with 'true world-cruising pedigree' but does not mention two full circumnavigations or World ARC specifically. The claim of 'two full circumnavigations with World ARC' is a significant factual assertion not confirmed by the cited source. This appears to be an embellishment of the vague broker marketing language.

The Cable Loft source page confirms they specialize in IP 485 rigging but explicitly provides no pricing and no 15-year replacement recommendation. The Calliope listing (the secondary source) returned an empty page with no data. The '$12,000 replacement estimate' and '15-year factory guidance' figures have no verifiable source in any page that loaded.

removed.key: refit.known_issues (exhaust-elbow leaks, chainplate labor, propane-locker drain)
removed.reason: Three specific issues in this consolidated fact are not supported by the cited sources. Life of Sailing article does NOT mention exhaust-elbow leaks, internal chainplate replacement procedure, or propane-locker drain design flaws. These details are cited as coming from Life of Sailing and the Sweet Dream listing, but neither source confirms them. They may be true (known in the community) but the sources cited do not support them.

The Calliope listing URL returned an empty template page with no vessel data. The specific claim that 'Calliope replaced standing rigging, headsail furlers, drums, lines (March 2023)' cannot be verified. The $12,000 figure also cannot be verified from any loaded source.

Cited sources are Calliope refit (empty page) and Landau Marine refit interview (video content, not extractable). No cost figure for in-mast furling service is verifiable from any source that loaded. Cost range appears estimated rather than sourced.

fabrication_flags: cold.watertight_bulkheads: The claim that 'internal frames are screwed (not bolted) into fiberglass backing plates with adhesive sealant per EU Category A standards' is cited from the Sweet Dream broker listing, which contains no such construction detail. This reads as a fabricated technical-sounding assertion with false source attribution.

tropical.ventilation / tropical.airflow: Exact counts of '15 stainless steel opening ports, 6 opening overhead hatches, 7 large fixed saloon windows, 3 opening galley ports' are presented with high confidence (0.92/0.9) but the cited source (yachtworld.com) returned 403 Forbidden and no other source confirms these counts. Suspiciously precise numbers without verifiable backing.

ergonomics.side_decks: Quoted description ('nice balance of traction/comfort underfoot while easy to clean') with specific color description ('slightly darker beige than gloss waterways/trunk cabin') reads as a fabricated reviewer quote. The boats.com source returned 403 and the quote style matches AI-generated review language rather than a real publication.

heavy_weather.storm_report: 'Two full circumnavigations with World ARC' is not stated in the cited source. The Sweet Dream listing uses vague broker language ('proven circumnavigator') — the specific detail of two circumnavigations and World ARC participation appears to be an AI-generated embellishment.

refit.standing_rigging_age: '$12,000 replacement estimate' and '15-year factory guidance' are stated with 0.85 confidence but neither figure appears on the Cable Loft page (no pricing) or the Calliope listing (empty page). These figures appear fabricated or extrapolated without a real source.

ergonomics.mob_recovery: 'Kato davits with integrated winch' is cited from the Sweet Dream listing but is not present in that listing. Specific equipment name appears inserted from elsewhere without source support.

stability.avs: The record presents YachtDatabase-computed ratios (D/L 245, SA/D 15.81) while simultaneously claiming a 40% ballast ratio — but YachtDatabase shows 36% ballast ratio. The numbers come from the same page but only the ones that look good are selected, while the less favorable ballast ratio from the same source is ignored. This selective quoting inflates the apparent stability credentials.
verify.verdict: Partially reliable. The core dimensional and ballast specs (LOA, beam, draft, displacement, ballast weight, engine, CE Category A, keel type, refrigeration brand, watermaker models, electrical refit details on Sweet Dream) are well-supported by verifiable sources. However, roughly one-third of the facts contain material problems: exact ventilation counts (15 ports, 6 hatches) cannot be verified as the primary source returned 403; the 'two World ARC circumnavigations' claim is an embellishment of vague broker copy; the $12,000 standing rigging cost and 15-year factory replacement guidance have no verifiable source; the watertight bulkhead construction detail (screwed vs. bolted frames) appears fabricated with false source attribution; the ballast ratio is inconsistently reported (36% vs 41%) and the record cherry-picks figures from two conflicting sources; and several quoted 'reviewer' descriptions of side decks and MOB gear read as AI-generated language not traceable to any real publication. The record is usable for broad characterization of the IP 485 but should not be trusted for any specific numerical claims (cost estimates, exact ventilation counts, stability ratios, refit costs) without independent re-verification."

cold_score.rationale: Cold-weather readiness rests on what could be confirmed. Cabin heating is verified — an engine heat-exchanger circuit plus a 220V immersion element. Tankage is exceptional for high-latitude self-sufficiency: roughly 300 gallons of fuel and 300–340 gallons of water. Against that, there is no inside or sheltered steering position — the center cockpit is exposed, with only a bimini and dodger rather than a hard shelter — and hull insulation, the keel-step configuration and any watertight bulkhead could not be confirmed from available sources. Confidence is moderate, reflecting that about a third of the cold-weather checklist remains unverified.
cold_score.basis: Heating: cold.heating (pjyachts.com, curtisstokes.net); Inside helm absent: cold.inside_helm (curtisstokes.net); Hard shelter partial: tropical.shade (tecsew.com); Cold tankage: performance.tankage (cruisingworld.com specs, iphomeport.com)

tropic_score.rationale: Tropical readiness is solid where it counts. Ventilation runs through four confirmed Dorade vents, though exact port and hatch counts are not confirmed in available sources. Shade comes from a bimini and dodger (Tecsew). Refrigeration is a real strength — a dual Frigoboat top-loading fridge and freezer documented on multiple listings. A watermaker is optional rather than standard, so a buyer cannot assume one is fitted. Light-air performance is the weak point: published SA/D figures conflict (17.6 vs 15.81) and the heavy ~39,000 lb displacement limits drive in light wind, though the cutter rig's staysail and yankee add flexibility. Confidence is reduced by the missing ventilation specifics and the disputed sail-area figures.
tropic_score.basis: Refrigeration: tropical.refrigeration (pjyachts.com, curtisstokes.net); Shade: tropical.shade (tecsew.com); Watermaker: tropical.watermaker (pjyachts.com, curtisstokes.net); Airflow partial: pjyachts.com dorade count; SA/D: removed (cruisingworld.com and yachtdatabase.com conflict); Rig type: heavy_weather.cutter_staysail (wellfoundyachts.com, curtisstokes.net)

stability_score.rationale: No Angle of Vanishing Stability figure is published for this boat in any available source, so the rating leans on the next-best evidence. CE Category A (Ocean) is confirmed — a Grabau International listing quotes the certification directly — which means the design meets the European standard for unrestricted offshore use. That is reassuring but not a substitute for a measured righting-arm limit: there is no published AVS, no STIX, and no usable sail-area-to-displacement cross-check (the two SA/D figures on record conflict). Ballast ratio sits in a healthy 0.36–0.41 range and the full-foil encapsulated keel with no external bolts is a structural plus, though neither fixes the angular stability limit. Confidence is moderate, reflecting the absence of a directly measured stability number.
stability_score.basis: stability.ce_category (grabauinternational.com — CE Category A certified, confidence 0.9); stability.avs (not published, confirmed by yachtdatabase.com cross-check); stability.stix (not published); stability.keel_type (pjyachts.com — full foil encapsulated lead)

storm_verdict.text: Insufficient sourced data. The only heavy-weather characterization in the verified record is vague broker marketing language ('proven circumnavigator,' 'true world-cruising pedigree') from the Curtis Stokes Sweet Dream listing — this is not a sourced storm or passage report. The claim of two World ARC circumnavigations was flagged as an embellishment of that broker copy and removed from the verified facts. The Cruising World review references the design philosophy of the IP 485 as a heavy-displacement bluewater cruiser but no storm performance data, sea-state limits, or documented incident accounts were accessible in any source that loaded. No owner forum posts with specific heavy-weather accounts, no delivery logs, and no offshore race or rally reports appear in the verified record.

by_item.system: Standing rigging (wire, turnbuckles, chainplates, inner forestay)
by_item.note: 19-20 yr boat is past the 15-20 yr interval. Cutter rig adds inner forestay and staysail stay. Cable Loft confirmed they specialize in IP 485 rigging. Upper end reflects full chainplate inspection and re-bed. Cost range is our estimate — no sourced price figure verified.
Past 8-10 yr service interval at ~19-20 yrs. In-mast furling adds dedicated furling line that is subject to wear.

by_item.system: Sails (in-mast main, yankee headsail, self-tacking staysail)
by_item.note: Three-sail cutter inventory confirmed (North Sails yankee + staysail documented, Mack Sails in-mast furling main documented). At 19-20 yrs all sails likely past 8-12 yr interval. In-mast main is more expensive to replace than standard due to Leisure Furl or equivalent batten-less requirement.

Major service (belts, impeller, hoses, heat exchanger, zincs, alignment) at low end. High end covers partial repower scenario if engine hours are high (no hours figure in verified record). Full repower not assumed until hours confirmed — standard interval is 25-30 yr or ~8,000-10,000 hrs.

by_item.system: Electronics (chartplotter, AIS, radar, VHF, autopilot, instruments)
by_item.note: 19-20 yr boat is past the 10-15 yr electronics interval. Raymarine systems documented but generation varies widely by vessel. Full navigation suite replacement assumed; high end includes radar, AIS transponder, SSB/Iridium, and autopilot controller upgrade.

by_item.system: Battery bank and charging (house + start batteries)
by_item.note: At 19-20 yrs any original AGM bank is well past 6-10 yr life. Sweet Dream had 6x Group-27 Lifeline AGMs replaced 2022-2023 — a refit buyer should budget for this regardless. High end adds lithium upgrade with BMS and shore-power charger replacement.
Age-appropriate replacement of galvanized or worn chain; windlass service or replacement. Rocna anchor documented on Sweet Dream. IP 485 needs robust tackle given 39,000 lb displacement.
19-20 yr vessel approaching but not yet at 25 yr full replacement interval. Budget for inspection, service, and replacement of any galled or corroded valves. Full haul-out required.

by_item.system: Liferaft and safety gear (flares, EPIRB, harnesses, tether)
by_item.note: Safety equipment is time-critical (flare expiry, EPIRB battery, liferaft hydrostatic release). No service dates in verified record. Full passage-ready safety kit budgeted.

by_item.system: Canvas (bimini, dodger, sail covers, cockpit enclosure)
by_item.note: Bimini and dodger confirmed present (Tecsew custom product + Curtis Stokes listing). At 19-20 yrs all canvas past the 10-15 yr interval. Replacement bimini and dodger set for a 48-ft center cockpit cutter runs at high end of range.

Watermaker is optional, not standard. If present (documented on some vessels at 60 L/hr Dessalator or 15 GPH Aqua Base), service/membrane replacement only ($1k-$3k). If absent, full installation budgeted at $6k-$15k. Range shown as 0 low because some vessels already have it.

by_item.system: Aluminum fuel tank inspection and possible replacement
by_item.note: IP 485-specific documented issue per IPYOA forum — factory replacement program ~$10,000. Low end covers professional inspection + cleaning + treatment if corrosion is early-stage. High end covers full replacement per factory program estimate. Water and holding tanks are fiberglass (not aluminum) per verified source — only fuel tank at risk.

by_item.system: Electrical system (contacts, wiring, switches, fuse boxes)
by_item.note: Salt-air corrosion of contacts, fuse boxes, light sockets, and wiring is a documented common IP issue per Life of Sailing. Range covers inspection, re-termination, and replacement of corroded components. High end covers partial rewire of engine room and nav station.

Documented model-specific issue — some builds used screws instead of bolts for port lights causing fiberglass cracking. Inspection and re-fastening or replacement with properly bolted units. High end includes any fiberglass crack repair.

refit_projection.assumptions: Age basis: production run 2005-2016; applying rubric to a representative ~2006-2008 vessel at 18-20 years old as of 2026. All cost ranges are our estimates — no specific cost figures for IP 485 standing rigging, sails, or in-mast furling service were verified from sourced pages (Cable Loft page has no pricing; Calliope listing returned empty page; Landau Marine was video content). Engine repower not assumed at this age; service budget only unless hours are confirmed high. Watermaker low end set to $0 because it is optional and may already be present. Deck core water intrusion (documented issue) not separately line-itemed because severity is survey-dependent — a surveyor finding soft spots would add $5k-$25k to the projection. Factory closure (March 2016) means parts sourcing relies on islandpacketparts.com / Mack Yacht Services for IP-specific items.

provisioning_specific.item: Aluminum fuel tank condition report before any offshore passage
provisioning_specific.why: Documented IP 485-specific corrosion issue confirmed by IPYOA forum; factory offered a ~$10,000 replacement program. At 19-20 yrs, tank integrity cannot be assumed without inspection. Water pooling at the bottom of the tank accelerates internal corrosion — a compromised tank offshore creates both fuel-contamination and fire risk.

provisioning_specific.item: Spare Frigoboat compressor (or full rebuild kit) for both fridge and freezer units
provisioning_specific.why: Dual Frigoboat top-loading units are confirmed standard equipment. Frigoboat compressors are proprietary and specific parts can be difficult to source outside Europe and major US marinas. With two units running, the statistical likelihood of a compressor failure on passage justifies carrying one spare unit or a comprehensive rebuild kit (valves, start capacitor, relay).

provisioning_specific.item: Hoyt self-tacking boom service parts (pivot bearings, traveler car, control line)
provisioning_specific.why: Hoyt self-tacking boom on the inner staysail is confirmed (Curtis Stokes Sweet Dream listing). The Hoyt system uses specific bearings and traveler components that are not interchangeable with generic hardware. Hoyt Manufacturing availability has historically been inconsistent; carry spares for the pivot points and traveler car before passage.

provisioning_specific.item: In-mast furling system spares: lower drum seal kit, furling line, masthead swivel inspection
provisioning_specific.why: In-mast furling main confirmed (Mack Sails in-mast furling documented). In-mast systems are the most failure-critical sail system on the boat — a jammed furler offshore leaves the mainsail either fully deployed or fully stowed with no intermediate option. Carry drum seal kit, a full-length spare furling line, and confirm masthead swivel condition before departure.

provisioning_specific.item: Electrical contact cleaner, marine-grade tinned wire, heat-shrink terminals (bulk supply)
provisioning_specific.why: Salt-air corrosion of contacts, fuse boxes, and wiring is a documented, common IP issue (Life of Sailing). On a 19-20 yr boat, any electrical fault offshore is likely corrosion-related. Bulk supply of Deoxit D5, tinned 16-18 AWG wire, and sealed butt connectors allows field repair of most switch and instrument failures without marina access.

provisioning_specific.item: Source parts account at islandpacketparts.com (Mack Yacht Services, Stuart FL) before departure
provisioning_specific.why: Island Packet factory closed March 2016. islandpacketparts.com (operated by Mack Yacht Services / Bellatrix Marine LLC) is the confirmed successor parts source for hard-to-find IP-specific items including chainplate kits. Establish an account and pre-order any known-needed IP-specific parts (chainplates, deck hardware, companionway seals) before leaving for a remote cruising area.

provisioning_specific.item: Cutter rig inner forestay (staysail stay) spare toggle and turnbuckle
provisioning_specific.why: Cutter configuration with inner forestay confirmed (wellfoundyachts.com, curtisstokes.net). The staysail stay and its attachment hardware are the third rig component and carry significant load when the staysail is deployed in heavy air — which is exactly when the cutter rig is most useful. A failed toggle on the inner stay offshore would require immediate improvisation. Carry a matching spare toggle and turnbuckle sized to the stay.

provisioning_specific.item: Port light fastener inspection and replacement kit (stainless M5/M6 bolts with backing plates)
provisioning_specific.why: Documented IP 485 model-specific defect — some builds used screws instead of through-bolts for port light installation, causing fiberglass cracking under dynamic loads (Life of Sailing). Before bluewater passage, inspect all port light fasteners. Carry replacement stainless bolts with appropriately sized backing plates sized to the IP 485 port light frame dimensions for any that need correction at sea.
